article.title: Characterization of novel regulators for heat stress tolerance in tomato from Indian sub‐continent
article.license: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.

events.event_label: NCED6 and NCED9 contribute to thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s006: Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s007: When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a). PMC7540533.p029.s008: This is in accordance with the phenotype observed in the tomato null mutant of notabilis that is deficient in ABA synthesis (Burbidge et al.,; Thompson et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s009: Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s010: It is known that high ABA levels cause ROS outburst.

events.event_label: HS upregulates HSF transcription factors
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s001: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family. PMC7540533.p022.s002: Seven tomato HSFs (A1b, A2, A3, A4c, A5, A7 and C1) exhibited HS‐mediated up‐regulation in both the cultivars with highest up‐regulation for HSFA2 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s003: We noted that the repressor class B HSF members, namely HSFB1, B2a and B2b, are up‐regulated only in the sensitive CA4. PMC7540533.p022.s004: Whether this has any direct correlation with CA4 being HS sensitive needs to be further evaluated.

events.event_label: ASAT catalyzes conversion of sucrose and acyl‑CoA to acylsugars in glandular trichome tip cells
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s001: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s002: Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

events.event_label: PI‑II inhibits trypsin and chymotrypsin
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s001: Furthermore, another gene but a ‘sensitive‐up gene’ which is Solyc03g020030 was functionally characterized. PMC7540533.p031.s002: This belongs to the proteinase inhibitor‐II (PI‐II) serine‐PI family having dimeric domains in the protein, inhibiting trypsin and chymotrypsin (Tamhane et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s003: The PI‐IIs are well documented in plant defence against insects, bacteria and fungi (Rehman et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s004: Few reports in abiotic stress tolerance like drought, salinity, osmotic variations and pH are also documented (Huang et al.,; Shan et al.,; Srinivasan et al.,).

events.event_label: Notabilis regulates ABA biosynthesis, correlates with water stress, and ABA influences thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s001: We then functionally validated the other tolerant‐up gene, Solyc07g056570 or Notabilis (9‐cis‐epoxycarotenoid dioxygenase 1). PMC7540533.p029.s002: Notabilis regulates the rate‐limiting step in the ABA biosynthesis pathway and has important role in water stress tolerance as evidenced by several reports (Burbidge et al.,; He et al.,; Luchi et al.,; Qin and Zeevaart,; Wan and Li,). PMC7540533.p029.s003: In Arabidopsis, ABA‐deficient and signalling mutants are involved in acquired thermotolerance acquisition (Larkindale and Huang,). PMC7540533.p029.s004: Arabidopsis thaliana NGATHA1 induces ABA biosynthesis by activating Notabilis homolog NCED3 gene during dehydration stress (Sato et al.,).

events.event_label: ASAT enzymes catalyze production of Acylsugars from sucrose and acyl CoA in tip cells of glandular trichomes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s001: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s002: Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

events.event_label: HS modulates expression of multiple transcription factor families
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p020.s012: Moreover, 26 TFs showed HS‐mediated strong repression (FC ≤ 5) in both cultivars (Figure 3c). PMC7540533.p020.s013: While Solyc08g008280 (WRKY‐53) was the top repressed candidate in CLN, Solyc10g005080 (late elongated hypocotyl) was highly down‐regulated TF in CA4 under HS (Figure 3c). PMC7540533.p020.s014: In addition, other TFs exhibiting strong repression belong to TF families bHLH (4), bZIP (2), C2H2 (3), MYB (3), TCP (2), WRKY (2) and YABBY (2) (Figure 3c and Table S4). PMC7540533.p020.s015: The highly HS‐responsive TF families with more than 20 of its members significantly modulated (up‐regulated and down‐regulated) at transcript level include bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, C3H, ERF, GRAS, HD‐ZIP, HSF, MYB, NAC and WRKY (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s016: Further, we scanned for those TF families that had at least two differentially regulated members in response to HS and also had ≥80% of its members exhibiting either up‐ or down‐regulation.

events.event_label: Notabilis overexpression improves thermotolerance and hypocotyl length
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s015: Overexpression of wheat TaNCED gene in Arabidopsis enhanced tolerance to drought stress by reducing stomatal conductance and transpiration (Tong et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s016: Further proof for Notabilis as a gene imparting thermotolerance was provided from tomato plants overexpressing Notabilis as judged by better seedling survival percentage and hypocotyl length in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g,h). PMC7540533.p029.s017: We then measured the relative abundance of HS marker genes in the silenced Notabilis plants; the level of all the genes was reduced several folds upon HS in comparison with control (Figure 4i). PMC7540533.p029.s018: Moreover, when we checked the expression of these HSR genes in response to ABA, they were all up‐regulated, suggesting Notabilis as an upstream regulator of these HS marker genes (Figure 4i). PMC7540533.p029.s019: Of these HSFs, HSFA2a and HSFA7a were down‐regulated the most and appeared as the critical candidates in the Notabilis‐ABA pathway‐mediated thermotolerance.

events.event_label: Notabilis (NCED) contributes to thermotolerance through ABA production, together with NCED6 and NCED9
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s006: Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s007: When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a). PMC7540533.p029.s008: This is in accordance with the phenotype observed in the tomato null mutant of notabilis that is deficient in ABA synthesis (Burbidge et al.,; Thompson et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s009: Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s010: It is known that high ABA levels cause ROS outburst.

events.event_label: HS regulates ERF.F4, ERF.F5 and ERF.C1, which participate in heat stress response
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s012: Of these, 12 ERFs were similarly regulated (9 up‐regulated and 3‐regulated down) in both cultivars, while others were regulated in cultivar‐biased manner. PMC7540533.p022.s013: We selected three previously uncharacterized ERFs (ERF.F4, ERF.F5, and ERF.C1) in HS response based on the observation that they all maintained high levels in CLN and low levels in CA4 under HS. PMC7540533.p022.s014: This was validated by qRT‐PCR also (Figure 3e). PMC7540533.p022.s015: These 3 ERFs are reported in fruit development (Hao et al.,; Di Matteo et al.,) in tomato, but their role in thermotolerance was lacking. PMC7540533.p022.s016: Functional characterization of the above three ERFs by transient overexpression significantly enhanced (more than doubled) the tomato seedling survival rate under HS as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3f) with highest survival percentage for ERF.F5.

events.event_label: ASAT activity influences ROS accumulation, photosynthesis, water‑use efficiency and seedling performance under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s009: These parameters are routinely adopted to assess thermotolerance of plants. PMC7540533.p026.s010: The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p026.s011: Gas exchange parameters measured by LI‐COR 6400 portable photosynthesis measuring system at the end of the heat treatment of ASAT‐silenced plants showed significant drop in net photosynthesis rate and water‐use efficiency (WUEi) as well as enhanced stomatal conductance and transpiration rate (Figure 4c–f) highlighting the role of ASAT as a positive regulator of thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p026.s012: This conclusion was further strengthened by assessing seedling performance under HS when ASAT gene was transiently overexpressed. PMC7540533.p026.s013: The seedling survival increased by nearly 17% in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g), and the reduction in hypocotyl length under HS was also around 8% in overexpression plants in comparison with 18% in vector control plants (Figure 4h).

events.event_label: ABA and Notabilis activate heat‑stress response genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s015: Overexpression of wheat TaNCED gene in Arabidopsis enhanced tolerance to drought stress by reducing stomatal conductance and transpiration (Tong et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s016: Further proof for Notabilis as a gene imparting thermotolerance was provided from tomato plants overexpressing Notabilis as judged by better seedling survival percentage and hypocotyl length in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g,h). PMC7540533.p029.s017: We then measured the relative abundance of HS marker genes in the silenced Notabilis plants; the level of all the genes was reduced several folds upon HS in comparison with control (Figure 4i). PMC7540533.p029.s018: Moreover, when we checked the expression of these HSR genes in response to ABA, they were all up‐regulated, suggesting Notabilis as an upstream regulator of these HS marker genes (Figure 4i). PMC7540533.p029.s019: Of these HSFs, HSFA2a and HSFA7a were down‐regulated the most and appeared as the critical candidates in the Notabilis‐ABA pathway‐mediated thermotolerance.

events.event_label: Overexpression of ERF.F4 and ERF.F5 improves seedling survival, hypocotyl length and total seedling weight under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s015: These 3 ERFs are reported in fruit development (Hao et al.,; Di Matteo et al.,) in tomato, but their role in thermotolerance was lacking. PMC7540533.p022.s016: Functional characterization of the above three ERFs by transient overexpression significantly enhanced (more than doubled) the tomato seedling survival rate under HS as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3f) with highest survival percentage for ERF.F5. PMC7540533.p022.s017: Seedlings overexpressing ERF.F4 and F5 exhibited higher hypocotyl length as well as shoot, root and total seedling weight as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3g,h). PMC7540533.p022.s018: Our data on TF regulation dynamics highlight role of TF families other than the undisputed relevance of HSFs in HS response. PMC7540533.p022.s019: These TFs could be promising candidates for enhancing tomato thermotolerance as we demonstrated for the three ERFs that function as positive regulators of HS response.

events.event_label: HSFA1 regulates HSFBs which repress HSFA1 activity and the heat shock response
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s009: These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s010: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s011: Another critical regulator of thermotolerance is the C‐REPEAT BINDING FACTOR2 (RCF2)‐regulated NAC019 that acts as an upstream regulator of HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1 (Guan et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s012: Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s013: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,).

events.event_label: Proteinase inhibitors and HXXXD‑type acyltransferases contribute to plant defence
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p014.s009: Solyc01g065530 (protein COBRA), Solyc01g081250 (glutathione s‐transferase), Solyc01g087020 (transmembrane protein), Solyc01g095140 (late embryogenesis abundant protein), Solyc01g107780 (glycosyltransferase), Solyc03g116890 (WRKY transcription factor 39), Soly05g052670, Solyc05g052680 and Solyc09g014280 (all three HXXXD‐type acyltransferase family proteins), Solyc06g049070 (nucleotide/sugar transporter family protein), Solyc07g008103 (blue copper protein), Solyc07g056570 (Notabilis), Solyc07g064410 (Kua‐ubiquitin conjugating enzyme hybrid), Solyc09g075920 (serine/threonine‐protein kinase), Solyc09g092500 (glycosyltransferase) and Solyc10g081570 (Marmande), were up‐regulated in CLN but down‐regulated in CA4 upon HS. PMC7540533.p014.s010: Gene ontology (GO) enrichment of these antagonistic genes highlighted the significant enrichment of negative regulation of endopeptidase, peptidase and proteolysis, response to water, pollination and transferase activity (Figure 2c). PMC7540533.p014.s011: Among these, five (the two proteinase inhibitors and the three HXXXD‐type acyltransferases) have roles in plant defence. PMC7540533.p014.s012: The qRT‐PCR validation of 10 genes selected from the above clusters confirmed the opposite HS regulation in CA4 and CLN (Figure 2d).

events.event_label: HS alters expression of Kunitz PI and Solyc03g020030
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s003: The PI‐IIs are well documented in plant defence against insects, bacteria and fungi (Rehman et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s004: Few reports in abiotic stress tolerance like drought, salinity, osmotic variations and pH are also documented (Huang et al.,; Shan et al.,; Srinivasan et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s005: Only a Kunitz PI (another class of serine‐PI family) has been shown to be induced in response to heat stress (Annamalai and Yanagihara,), however, information regarding PI‐II‐class role in HS is completely lacking. PMC7540533.p031.s006: Solyc03g020030 was expressed at high levels in CLN under control condition but decreased up to threefold under HS, while significant increase was noticed in CA4 under HS. PMC7540533.p031.s007: We find there is enrichment of GO term of ‘serine‐type endopeptidase inhibitor activity’ in CA4‐specific up‐regulation (Figure 2c).

events.event_label: ERF.F4 and ERF.F5 enhance hypocotyl length
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s015: These 3 ERFs are reported in fruit development (Hao et al.,; Di Matteo et al.,) in tomato, but their role in thermotolerance was lacking. PMC7540533.p022.s016: Functional characterization of the above three ERFs by transient overexpression significantly enhanced (more than doubled) the tomato seedling survival rate under HS as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3f) with highest survival percentage for ERF.F5. PMC7540533.p022.s017: Seedlings overexpressing ERF.F4 and F5 exhibited higher hypocotyl length as well as shoot, root and total seedling weight as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3g,h). PMC7540533.p022.s018: Our data on TF regulation dynamics highlight role of TF families other than the undisputed relevance of HSFs in HS response. PMC7540533.p022.s019: These TFs could be promising candidates for enhancing tomato thermotolerance as we demonstrated for the three ERFs that function as positive regulators of HS response.

events.event_label: Notabilis overexpression enhances thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s015: Overexpression of wheat TaNCED gene in Arabidopsis enhanced tolerance to drought stress by reducing stomatal conductance and transpiration (Tong et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s016: Further proof for Notabilis as a gene imparting thermotolerance was provided from tomato plants overexpressing Notabilis as judged by better seedling survival percentage and hypocotyl length in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g,h). PMC7540533.p029.s017: We then measured the relative abundance of HS marker genes in the silenced Notabilis plants; the level of all the genes was reduced several folds upon HS in comparison with control (Figure 4i). PMC7540533.p029.s018: Moreover, when we checked the expression of these HSR genes in response to ABA, they were all up‐regulated, suggesting Notabilis as an upstream regulator of these HS marker genes (Figure 4i). PMC7540533.p029.s019: Of these HSFs, HSFA2a and HSFA7a were down‐regulated the most and appeared as the critical candidates in the Notabilis‐ABA pathway‐mediated thermotolerance.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p020.s009: Of these, 3 TFs, viz. PMC7540533.p020.s011: The plot of above 30 TFs clearly revealed that the heat‐tolerant CLN showed higher magnitude of HS‐mediated induction in majority of the TFs and common key TF pool was activated upon HS in both cultivars (Figure 3b). PMC7540533.p020.s012: Moreover, 26 TFs showed HS‐mediated strong repression (FC ≤ 5) in both cultivars (Figure 3c). PMC7540533.p020.s013: While Solyc08g008280 (WRKY‐53) was the top repressed candidate in CLN, Solyc10g005080 (late elongated hypocotyl) was highly down‐regulated TF in CA4 under HS (Figure 3c). PMC7540533.p020.s014: In addition, other TFs exhibiting strong repression belong to TF families bHLH (4), bZIP (2), C2H2 (3), MYB (3), TCP (2), WRKY (2) and YABBY (2) (Figure 3c and Table S4). PMC7540533.p020.s015: The highly HS‐responsive TF families with more than 20 of its members significantly modulated (up‐regulated and down‐regulated) at transcript level include bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, C3H, ERF, GRAS, HD‐ZIP, HSF, MYB, NAC and WRKY (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s016: Further, we scanned for those TF families that had at least two differentially regulated members in response to HS and also had ≥80% of its members exhibiting either up‐ or down‐regulation.

events.event_label: HS induces ROS accumulation and cell death
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s009: Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s010: It is known that high ABA levels cause ROS outburst. PMC7540533.p029.s011: However, the leaves of Notabilis‐silenced plants show slightly increased ROS levels and HS‐induced cell death in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p029.s012: This apparent anomaly may be attributed to a balance between low ROS levels due to reduced ABA levels and other pathways contributing to ROS production in response to HS. PMC7540533.p029.s013: The gas exchange analyses further supported the important role of Notabilis in thermotolerance.

events.event_label: Heat response localized in tomato leaves
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s009: Till now, the knowledge about the effect of HS on tomato leaf by exploiting the comparative transcriptomics in contrasting cultivars is not well understood. PMC7540533.p006.s010: Tomato is increasingly becoming a cash crop in regions of higher temperatures (the tropics and sub‐tropics) than the optimum 26/20 °C during the photoperiod/dark period (Srivastava et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s011: One way for developing HS‐resilient crops is to exploit the naturally evolved mechanistic signalling frameworks in cultivars exhibiting contrasting response to HS (Challinor et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s012: To uncover the HS‐responsive molecular mechanisms in tomato using naturally occurring cultivars growing in the sub‐tropical climatic conditions of the Indian sub‐continent, we first evaluated nine tolerant/sensitive tomato cultivars under HS to identify the best contrasting pair, viz. PMC7540533.p006.s013: CLN1621L (henceforth referred to as CLN, tolerant) and CA4 (sensitive). PMC7540533.p006.s014: Comparative analysis of the heat‐responsive transcriptomic signatures of leaves highlighted conserved to genotype‐specific reprogramming at transcriptional levels. PMC7540533.p006.s015: Functional assessment by silencing (virus‐induced gene silencing, VIGS) and transient overexpression of three previously uncharacterized genes, that exhibit antagonistic expression in the contrasting cultivars under HS, established their roles as positive/negative regulators of HS tolerance. PMC7540533.p006.s016: Promoter:reporter expression analysis highlights that cultivar‐dependent transcription factors govern the opposite expression of these genes.

events.event_label: ASAT knockdown reduces WUEi and causes leaf drooping
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s006: Here, we report yet unexplored function of an ASAT member in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p026.s007: Plants exhibiting successful knock‐down of Solyc09g014280 transcripts (Figure S11) showed severe drooping of leaves upon recovery after being exposed to HS in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4a). PMC7540533.p026.s008: Heat stress results in a higher than optimal concentration of ROS that not only affect plant’s ability to photosynthesize but also cause cell death. PMC7540533.p026.s009: These parameters are routinely adopted to assess thermotolerance of plants. PMC7540533.p026.s010: The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p026.s011: Gas exchange parameters measured by LI‐COR 6400 portable photosynthesis measuring system at the end of the heat treatment of ASAT‐silenced plants showed significant drop in net photosynthesis rate and water‐use efficiency (WUEi) as well as enhanced stomatal conductance and transpiration rate (Figure 4c–f) highlighting the role of ASAT as a positive regulator of thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p026.s012: This conclusion was further strengthened by assessing seedling performance under HS when ASAT gene was transiently overexpressed. PMC7540533.p026.s013: The seedling survival increased by nearly 17% in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g), and the reduction in hypocotyl length under HS was also around 8% in overexpression plants in comparison with 18% in vector control plants (Figure 4h).

events.event_label: Heat stress activates HSR, which is repressed by HSFBs
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s003: High temperature affects the phenological development, photosynthesis, respiration, initiation, expansion and senescence of plant organs (Wang et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s004: As sessile life forms, it is imperative for plants to evolve mechanisms to adapt to the thermal stress to minimize damage on their growth and reproduction. PMC7540533.p005.s005: This requires dynamic reprogramming at the level of transcriptome, proteome, metabolome, lipidome and epigenome leading to the activation of complex heat stress response (HSR) (Mittler et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s006: It is therefore necessary to have in‐depth understanding of the plant thermotolerance mechanisms at molecular level to achieve sustainable food production. PMC7540533.p005.s007: The canonical HSR involves HS sensing by Phytochrome B (Jung et al.,), the heat‐induced modulations at the level of membrane stability activating the calcium and lipid signalling (Saidi et al.,), and the interplay of conserved heat stress transcription factor‐heat shock protein (HSF‐HSP) module. PMC7540533.p005.s009: These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s010: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s011: Another critical regulator of thermotolerance is the C‐REPEAT BINDING FACTOR2 (RCF2)‐regulated NAC019 that acts as an upstream regulator of HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1 (Guan et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s012: Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s013: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,).

events.event_label: HS affects leaf
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p015.s001: Heat stress‐responsive transcriptional modulations in leaf of tolerant, CLN and sensitive, CA4 tomato cultivars. PMC7540533.p015.s002: (a) The Venn diagram depicts the comparison of heat response of CLN and CA4 leaf transcriptome. PMC7540533.p015.s003: The genes with at least twofold up‐ or down‐regulation with FDR P‐value of ≤0.05 were considered. PMC7540533.p015.s004: The genes with strict cultivar‐specific HS response are written in red. PMC7540533.p015.s005: (b) The expression profile of cluster of genes with opposite heat‐mediated regulation in leaf of CLN and CA4 under control and heat conditions. PMC7540533.p015.s006: Scale depicts the log2‐transformed fold change. (d) The qRT‐PCR validation of selected genes with antagonistic HS response between CLN and CA4 in leaves.

events.event_label: HS triggers stress response pathways
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p016.s001: Around ~49% (3402 out of 6954) HS‐responsive genes followed conserved HS‐regulated gene expression; 1752 DEGs were up‐regulated and 1650 DEGs were down‐regulated in both cultivars (Figure 2a), signifying a core universal HS response. PMC7540533.p016.s002: GO enrichment analysis suggested the involvement of up‐regulated genes in cellular response to nutrient starvation specifically phosphate starvation in addition to protein folding (Figure S3a,b). PMC7540533.p016.s003: Furthermore, proteins belonging to chaperones, winged helix TFs, mRNA splicing and processing factors were significantly enriched among the conserved up‐regulated genes (Figure S3c). PMC7540533.p016.s004: KEGG pathways like protein processing in ER, spliceosome and ubiquitin‐mediated proteolysis were enriched in conserved up‐regulated genes (Table S3). PMC7540533.p016.s005: Many recent reports on stress‐responsive regulation of spliceosome per se, as well as, crucial role of alternatively spliced transcripts in response to abiotic stresses including HS have been reported in plants (Deng et al.,; Seo et al.,; Carrasco‐López et al.,; Calixto et al.,; Filichkin et al.,; Laloum et al.,; Huertas et al.,). events.event_label: ASAT modulation affects multiple physiological traits and thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s009: These parameters are routinely adopted to assess thermotolerance of plants. PMC7540533.p026.s010: The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p026.s011: Gas exchange parameters measured by LI‐COR 6400 portable photosynthesis measuring system at the end of the heat treatment of ASAT‐silenced plants showed significant drop in net photosynthesis rate and water‐use efficiency (WUEi) as well as enhanced stomatal conductance and transpiration rate (Figure 4c–f) highlighting the role of ASAT as a positive regulator of thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p026.s012: This conclusion was further strengthened by assessing seedling performance under HS when ASAT gene was transiently overexpressed. PMC7540533.p026.s013: The seedling survival increased by nearly 17% in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g), and the reduction in hypocotyl length under HS was also around 8% in overexpression plants in comparison with 18% in vector control plants (Figure 4h). PMC7540533.p026.s014: Moreover, the relative abundance of HS marker genes (HSFA2a/A7a/A3a, sHSP24.5 CI and HSP90) (Figure 4i) was reduced several folds in ASAT‐silenced plants reiterating the fact that ASAT has a key role in providing thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p026.s015: Further, to understand how the transcriptional regulation of ASAT results in antagonistic expression between the contrasting cultivars, we sequenced 1.5 kb promoter from both the cultivars and checked promoter ASAT:GUS–reporter expression by assaying CLN promoter:GUS–reporter in CA4 background and vice versa. PMC7540533.p026.s016: We found that there was one substitution of ‘T’ to ‘A’ in CA4 promoter, which disrupted the binding sites of two TFs, namely NF‐Y A/B/C and AP2 having cis‐binding sites, that is ACAAT and AATCAA, respectively, in CA4.

PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s002: This belongs to the proteinase inhibitor‐II (PI‐II) serine‐PI family having dimeric domains in the protein, inhibiting trypsin and chymotrypsin (Tamhane et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s003: The PI‐IIs are well documented in plant defence against insects, bacteria and fungi (Rehman et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s004: Few reports in abiotic stress tolerance like drought, salinity, osmotic variations and pH are also documented (Huang et al.,; Shan et al.,; Srinivasan et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s013: In contrast, VIGS‐based silencing of PI‐II in Pusa Ruby exhibited higher survival rate (Figure 5e–g). PMC7540533.p031.s014: Furthermore, enhanced expression of HS marker genes in PI‐II‐silenced plants confirmed its role as a negative regulator of HS tolerance (Figure 5h,i). PMC7540533.p031.s015: In tomato, SlbZIP1 TF (Solyc01g079480.3) transcriptionally regulates PI‐II (Zhu et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s016: When we checked our NGS data for a possible cultivar‐specific differential response of PI‐II by SlbZIP1 in response to HS, the TF was not significantly differentially regulated in CLN (1.85‐fold change) and CA4 (1.25‐fold change) (Table S2).

PMC7540533.p003.s004: The antagonistically expressing genes encode enzymes and proteins that have roles in plant defence and abiotic stresses.

relations.subject: thermo‐sensitivity

evidence.text: The footprint of tomato cultivation, a cool region crop that exhibits heat stress (HS) sensitivity, is increasing in the tropics/sub‐tropics.

PMC7540533.p003.s003: Comparative transcriptome‐wide signatures of a tolerant (CLN1621L) and sensitive (CA4) cultivar pair shortlisted from a pool of varieties exhibiting variable thermo‐sensitivity using physiological‐, survival‐ and yield‐related traits revealed redundant to cultivar‐specific HS regulation. Knowledge of novel regulatory hot spots from varieties growing in the Indian sub‐continent climatic zones could be vital for developing HS‐resilient crops.

Comparative transcriptome‐wide signatures of a tolerant (CLN1621L) and sensitive (CA4) cultivar pair shortlisted from a pool of varieties exhibiting variable thermo‐sensitivity using physiological‐, survival‐ and yield‐related traits revealed redundant to cultivar‐specific HS regulation.
The antagonistically expressing genes encode enzymes and proteins that have roles in plant defence and abiotic stresses.

relations.subject: heat stress

evidence.text: This requires dynamic reprogramming at the level of transcriptome, proteome, metabolome, lipidome and epigenome leading to the activation of complex heat stress response (HSR) (Mittler et al.,).

relations.evidence_preview: This requires dynamic reprogramming at the level of transcriptome, proteome, metabolome, lipidome and epigenome leading to the activation of complex heat stress response (HSR) (Mittler et al.,).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s003: High temperature affects the phenological development, photosynthesis, respiration, initiation, expansion and senescence of plant organs (Wang et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s004: As sessile life forms, it is imperative for plants to evolve mechanisms to adapt to the thermal stress to minimize damage on their growth and reproduction. PMC7540533.p005.s005: This requires dynamic reprogramming at the level of transcriptome, proteome, metabolome, lipidome and epigenome leading to the activation of complex heat stress response (HSR) (Mittler et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s006: It is therefore necessary to have in‐depth understanding of the plant thermotolerance mechanisms at molecular level to achieve sustainable food production. PMC7540533.p005.s007: The canonical HSR involves HS sensing by Phytochrome B (Jung et al.,), the heat‐induced modulations at the level of membrane stability activating the calcium and lipid signalling (Saidi et al.,), and the interplay of conserved heat stress transcription factor‐heat shock protein (HSF‐HSP) module.

evidence_context_sentences.text: 8 °C rise in the global mean temperatures has been projected by the end of this century (IPCC AR5, Pachauri et al., ).
High temperature affects the phenological development, photosynthesis, respiration, initiation, expansion and senescence of plant organs (Wang et al.,).
As sessile life forms, it is imperative for plants to evolve mechanisms to adapt to the thermal stress to minimize damage on their growth and reproduction.
This requires dynamic reprogramming at the level of transcriptome, proteome, metabolome, lipidome and epigenome leading to the activation of complex heat stress response (HSR) (Mittler et al.,).

relations.subject: HSFA1

evidence.text: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,).

relations.evidence_preview: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s009: These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s010: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s011: Another critical regulator of thermotolerance is the C‐REPEAT BINDING FACTOR2 (RCF2)‐regulated NAC019 that acts as an upstream regulator of HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1 (Guan et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s012: Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s013: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,).

evidence_context_sentences.text: The canonical HSR involves HS sensing by Phytochrome B (Jung et al.,), the heat‐induced modulations at the level of membrane stability activating the calcium and lipid signalling (Saidi et al.,), and the interplay of conserved heat stress transcription factor‐heat shock protein (HSF‐HSP) module.

Upon HS, HSFA1s, the master regulators of HSR, are relieved from repression by HSP70/90, relocate from cytoplasm to nucleus and activate other HSFs (HSFA7s, HSFA2 and HSFBs), dehydration‐responsive element‐binding protein 2A (DREB2A) and multiprotein bridging factor 1C (MBF1C) (Liu et al.,; Mishra et al.,; Yoshida et al.,).

These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,).
In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,).
Another critical regulator of thermotolerance is the C‐REPEAT BINDING FACTOR2 (RCF2)‐regulated NAC019 that acts as an upstream regulator of HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1 (Guan et al.,).

evidence.text: HS leads to reduced tomato yield by affecting its vegetative as well as reproductive development (Pressman et al.,; Sato et al.,).

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s001: Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum) is the second most cultivated vegetable in the world. PMC7540533.p006.s002: HS leads to reduced tomato yield by affecting its vegetative as well as reproductive development (Pressman et al.,; Sato et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s003: Transcript profiling using cDNA‐AFLP (Bita et al.,) and microarray analysis (Frank et al.,) as well as proteomic analyses (Mazzeo et al.,) of microspores has shown active involvement of HSPs, ROS scavengers, hormones, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p006.s004: Heat‐inducible tomato HSP21 protects PSII from the HS‐induced oxidative stress and also plays role in fruit ripening (Neta‐Sharir et al.,).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum) is the second most cultivated vegetable in the world.
HS leads to reduced tomato yield by affecting its vegetative as well as reproductive development (Pressman et al.,; Sato et al.,).

Transcript profiling using cDNA‐AFLP (Bita et al.,) and microarray analysis (Frank et al.,) as well as proteomic analyses (Mazzeo et al.,) of microspores has shown active involvement of HSPs, ROS scavengers, hormones, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation in tomato HS response.
Heat‐inducible tomato HSP21 protects PSII from the HS‐induced oxidative stress and also plays role in fruit ripening (Neta‐Sharir et al.,).

One way for developing HS‐resilient crops is to exploit the naturally evolved mechanistic signalling frameworks in cultivars exhibiting contrasting response to HS (Challinor et al.,).

evidence_context_sentences.text: One way for developing HS‐resilient crops is to exploit the naturally evolved mechanistic signalling frameworks in cultivars exhibiting contrasting response to HS (Challinor et al.,).

To uncover the HS‐responsive molecular mechanisms in tomato using naturally occurring cultivars growing in the sub‐tropical climatic conditions of the Indian sub‐continent, we first evaluated nine tolerant/sensitive tomato cultivars under HS to identify the best contrasting pair, viz.
CLN1621L (henceforth referred to as CLN, tolerant) and CA4 (sensitive).
Comparative analysis of the heat‐responsive transcriptomic signatures of leaves highlighted conserved to genotype‐specific reprogramming at transcriptional levels.

Functional assessment by silencing (virus‐induced gene silencing, VIGS) and transient overexpression of three previously uncharacterized genes, that exhibit antagonistic expression in the contrasting cultivars under HS, established their roles as positive/negative regulators of HS tolerance.

relations.subject: HS

evidence.text: Hierarchical clustering clearly divided the cultivars into four clades based on their HS response, viz. (i) most tolerant (CLN), (ii) moderately tolerant (Pusa Sadabahar, IIHR2274, Hisar Arun and IIHR2201), (iii) sensitive (Pusa Ruby, Pusa‐120 and Pusa Rohini) and (iv) highly sensitive (CA4) (Figure 1a).

This was further strengthened by PCA of all the above factors that showed the separation of each cultivar on PC1 (82.8%) based on the performance under HS, placing CLN and CA4 on the two extremities (Figure 1b), thus identifying them as the most tolerant and sensitive cultivars to HS, respectively.

relations.subject: HS

evidence.text: The genomic variations between different cultivars within the same species are often armed with specific mechanisms to tolerate stress at morphological, physiological and molecular levels (Huang and Gao,). Tomato production has considerably increased in the tropical and sub‐tropical regions (Nicola et al.,; Srivastava et al.,). To exploit the variability in thermotolerance, 9 tomato cultivars from Indian sub‐continent were screened and graded as tolerant and sensitive to shortlist best contrasting pair. These cultivars included five tolerant [CLN, IIHR2201, IIHR2274, Pusa Sadabahar and Hisar Arun] and four sensitive cultivars (CA4, Pusa Ruby, Pusa Rohini and Pusa120) (Chavan et al.,; Kartikeya et al.,; Meena and Bahadur,; Sangu et al.,). The tolerance of cultivars to HS was judged based on the parameters adopted by several previous reports for screening tomato cultivars under HS (Abdul‐Baki,; Abdul‐Baki and Stommel,). These include survival assays at seedling stage (Figure S1a) and at 1‐month‐old stage (Figure S1b); physiological assays like proline content, relative water content (RWC) and electrolytic leakage (EL) (Figure S1c–e); and yield‐related traits like percentage of fruit set per plant and total fruit yield (Figure S1f,g). Higher values for all the above parameters and lower values for EL are hallmarks of better thermotolerance. The cultivars were ranked for their performance by assaying all the above parameters as percentage increase/decrease under HS relative to control conditions. CLN displayed highest ranking among the cultivars for all the above parameters except EL (Figure 1a and Figure S1e). CA4 on the other hand had highest EL and obtained lowest rank for all other parameters except proline content where Pusa Ruby had least value (Figure 1a; Figure S1c). Of note, percentage survival of 1‐month‐old CLN plants was more than 80%, whereas it was mere 11% in CA4. Fruit set was also higher in CLN (81%) as compared to CA4 (27%), as reflected by nearly 50% yield being maintained in CLN but only 6% in CA4 (Figure S1f,g). Hierarchical clustering clearly divided the cultivars into four clades based on their HS response, viz. (i) most tolerant (CLN), (ii) moderately tolerant (Pusa Sadabahar, IIHR2274, Hisar Arun and IIHR2201), (iii) sensitive (Pusa Ruby, Pusa‐120 and Pusa Rohini) and (iv) highly sensitive (CA4) (Figure 1a). This was further strengthened by PCA of all the above factors that showed the separation of each cultivar on PC1 (82.8%) based on the performance under HS, placing CLN and CA4 on the two extremities (Figure 1b), thus identifying them as the most tolerant and sensitive cultivars to HS, respectively. CLN is reported to be tolerant to salt stress (Saeed et al.,) and TMV infection (https://avrdc.org). CLN has been shown to have higher plant vigour and pollen viability as compared to sensitive CA4 (Sangu et al.,). In addition, CLN is reported to maintain highest percentage of seeded fruits under HS as compared to several other cultivars (Comlekcioglu and Soylu,). The CLN and CA4 pair has been used as donor parents for generating mapping populations towards HS (https://avrdc.org). Thus, decoding the transcriptome of these two cultivars in HS response should provide valuable resource for future research to improve thermotolerance in tomato.

evidence.text: Around ~49% (3402 out of 6954) HS‐responsive genes followed conserved HS‐regulated gene expression; 1752 DEGs were up‐regulated and 1650 DEGs were down‐regulated in both cultivars (Figure 2a), signifying a core universal HS response.

HSFA2 (Solyc08g062960), and dehydration‐responsive element‐binding (DREB) TF (Solyc05g052410) were more than 100‐fold up‐regulated in both cultivars.

relations.subject: HS

evidence.text: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family.

relations.evidence_preview: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s001: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family. PMC7540533.p022.s002: Seven tomato HSFs (A1b, A2, A3, A4c, A5, A7 and C1) exhibited HS‐mediated up‐regulation in both the cultivars with highest up‐regulation for HSFA2 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s003: We noted that the repressor class B HSF members, namely HSFB1, B2a and B2b, are up‐regulated only in the sensitive CA4. PMC7540533.p022.s004: Whether this has any direct correlation with CA4 being HS sensitive needs to be further evaluated.

evidence_context_sentences.text: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family.
Seven tomato HSFs (A1b, A2, A3, A4c, A5, A7 and C1) exhibited HS‐mediated up‐regulation in both the cultivars with highest up‐regulation for HSFA2 (Table S4).
We noted that the repressor class B HSF members, namely HSFB1, B2a and B2b, are up‐regulated only in the sensitive CA4.

Whether this has any direct correlation with CA4 being HS sensitive needs to be further evaluated. HSFB1 is endowed with both co‐activator as well as repressor functions in tomato, while it acts as repressor in Arabidopsis (Bharti et al.,; Fragkostefanakis et al.,; Ikeda et al.,).

We noted that the repressor class B HSF members, namely HSFB1, B2a and B2b, are up‐regulated only in the sensitive CA4.

Whether this has any direct correlation with CA4 being HS sensitive needs to be further evaluated. HSFB1 is endowed with both co‐activator as well as repressor functions in tomato, while it acts as repressor in Arabidopsis (Bharti et al.,; Fragkostefanakis et al.,; Ikeda et al.,).

evidence_context_sentences.text: HSFA6a was up‐regulated, and HSFA4b was down‐regulated under HS only in CLN but not in CA4 (Table S4).
AtHSFA6a is induced in response to ABA, salt and drought and is responsible for the transcriptional activation of stress‐responsive gene DREB2a via ABA‐dependent signalling pathway (Hwang et al.,).
Furthermore, we investigated the ERF family that showed highest number of HS‐responsive TFs and include AP2, DREBs and ERFs (Sakuma et al.,).
Various ERFs are reported to mediate development and stress response, but only few address their regulatory role in thermotolerance (Licausi et al.,; Klay et al.,; Müller and Munné‐Bosch, ).
Out of 147 ERFs reported in tomato, 30 members followed differential HS response in CLN and CA4 (Table S4).

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s015: These 3 ERFs are reported in fruit development (Hao et al.,; Di Matteo et al.,) in tomato, but their role in thermotolerance was lacking. PMC7540533.p022.s016: Functional characterization of the above three ERFs by transient overexpression significantly enhanced (more than doubled) the tomato seedling survival rate under HS as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3f) with highest survival percentage for ERF.F5. PMC7540533.p022.s017: Seedlings overexpressing ERF.F4 and F5 exhibited higher hypocotyl length as well as shoot, root and total seedling weight as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3g,h). PMC7540533.p022.s018: Our data on TF regulation dynamics highlight role of TF families other than the undisputed relevance of HSFs in HS response. PMC7540533.p022.s019: These TFs could be promising candidates for enhancing tomato thermotolerance as we demonstrated for the three ERFs that function as positive regulators of HS response.

PMC7540533.p022.s016: Functional characterization of the above three ERFs by transient overexpression significantly enhanced (more than doubled) the tomato seedling survival rate under HS as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3f) with highest survival percentage for ERF.F5. PMC7540533.p022.s017: Seedlings overexpressing ERF.F4 and F5 exhibited higher hypocotyl length as well as shoot, root and total seedling weight as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3g,h). PMC7540533.p022.s018: Our data on TF regulation dynamics highlight role of TF families other than the undisputed relevance of HSFs in HS response. PMC7540533.p022.s019: These TFs could be promising candidates for enhancing tomato thermotolerance as we demonstrated for the three ERFs that function as positive regulators of HS response.

relations.subject: Solyc09g014280

evidence.text: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,).

relations.evidence_preview: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s001: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s002: Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,).

Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,).

Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,).
Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

relations.subject: ASAT gene

evidence.text: The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b).

relations.evidence_preview: The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s009: These parameters are routinely adopted to assess thermotolerance of plants. PMC7540533.p026.s010: The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p026.s011: Gas exchange parameters measured by LI‐COR 6400 portable photosynthesis measuring system at the end of the heat treatment of ASAT‐silenced plants showed significant drop in net photosynthesis rate and water‐use efficiency (WUEi) as well as enhanced stomatal conductance and transpiration rate (Figure 4c–f) highlighting the role of ASAT as a positive regulator of thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p026.s012: This conclusion was further strengthened by assessing seedling performance under HS when ASAT gene was transiently overexpressed. PMC7540533.p026.s013: The seedling survival increased by nearly 17% in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g), and the reduction in hypocotyl length under HS was also around 8% in overexpression plants in comparison with 18% in vector control plants (Figure 4h).

evidence_context_sentences.text: These parameters are routinely adopted to assess thermotolerance of plants.

The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b).

Gas exchange parameters measured by LI‐COR 6400 portable photosynthesis measuring system at the end of the heat treatment of ASAT‐silenced plants showed significant drop in net photosynthesis rate and water‐use efficiency (WUEi) as well as enhanced stomatal conductance and transpiration rate (Figure 4c–f) highlighting the role of ASAT as a positive regulator of thermotolerance.

This conclusion was further strengthened by assessing seedling performance under HS when ASAT gene was transiently overexpressed. The seedling survival increased by nearly 17% in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g), and the reduction in hypocotyl length under HS was also around 8% in overexpression plants in comparison with 18% in vector control plants (Figure 4h).
Moreover, the relative abundance of HS marker genes (HSFA2a/A7a/A3a, sHSP24.

PMC7540533.p026.s015: Further, to understand how the transcriptional regulation of ASAT results in antagonistic expression between the contrasting cultivars, we sequenced 1.5 kb promoter from both the cultivars and checked promoter ASAT:GUS–reporter expression by assaying CLN promoter:GUS–reporter in CA4 background and vice versa. PMC7540533.p026.s016: We found that there was one substitution of ‘T’ to ‘A’ in CA4 promoter, which disrupted the binding sites of two TFs, namely NF‐Y A/B/C and AP2 having cis‐binding sites, that is ACAAT and AATCAA, respectively, in CA4.

evidence_context_sentences.text: 1 QTL, associated with thermo‐inhibition of seed germination (Huo et al.,).
Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance.

When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a).
This is in accordance with the phenotype observed in the tomato null mutant of notabilis that is deficient in ABA synthesis (Burbidge et al.,; Thompson et al.,).
Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,).

Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance.

When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a).
This is in accordance with the phenotype observed in the tomato null mutant of notabilis that is deficient in ABA synthesis (Burbidge et al.,; Thompson et al.,).
Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,).

It is known that high ABA levels cause ROS outburst. However, the leaves of Notabilis‐silenced plants show slightly increased ROS levels and HS‐induced cell death in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4b).
This apparent anomaly may be attributed to a balance between low ROS levels due to reduced ABA levels and other pathways contributing to ROS production in response to HS.
The gas exchange analyses further supported the important role of Notabilis in thermotolerance.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Overexpression of wheat TaNCED gene in Arabidopsis enhanced tolerance to drought stress by reducing stomatal conductance and transpiration (Tong et al.,).

Further proof for Notabilis as a gene imparting thermotolerance was provided from tomato plants overexpressing Notabilis as judged by better seedling survival percentage and hypocotyl length in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g,h).
We then measured the relative abundance of HS marker genes in the silenced Notabilis plants; the level of all the genes was reduced several folds upon HS in comparison with control (Figure 4i).
Moreover, when we checked the expression of these HSR genes in response to ABA, they were all up‐regulated, suggesting Notabilis as an upstream regulator of these HS marker genes (Figure 4i).
Of these HSFs, HSFA2a and HSFA7a were down‐regulated the most and appeared as the critical candidates in the Notabilis‐ABA pathway‐mediated thermotolerance.

We then measured the relative abundance of HS marker genes in the silenced Notabilis plants; the level of all the genes was reduced several folds upon HS in comparison with control (Figure 4i).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Moreover, when we checked the expression of these HSR genes in response to ABA, they were all up‐regulated, suggesting Notabilis as an upstream regulator of these HS marker genes (Figure 4i).
Of these HSFs, HSFA2a and HSFA7a were down‐regulated the most and appeared as the critical candidates in the Notabilis‐ABA pathway‐mediated thermotolerance.
Furthermore, we asked whether the opposite expression of Notabilis in CLN‐CA4 cultivars is because of variation in promoter sequences and/or an inherent difference in TF pool of both the cultivars.
Sequence information of promoters for both the varieties highlighted no SNPs; thus, a regulatory influence of the cis‐sites in the contrasting expression was ruled out.
We then checked the expression of GUS gene hooked to Notabilis promoter in CLN and CA4 background.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Furthermore, another gene but a ‘sensitive‐up gene’ which is Solyc03g020030 was functionally characterized. This belongs to the proteinase inhibitor‐II (PI‐II) serine‐PI family having dimeric domains in the protein, inhibiting trypsin and chymotrypsin (Tamhane et al.,).
The PI‐IIs are well documented in plant defence against insects, bacteria and fungi (Rehman et al.,).
Few reports in abiotic stress tolerance like drought, salinity, osmotic variations and pH are also documented (Huang et al.,; Shan et al.,; Srinivasan et al.,).

Only a Kunitz PI (another class of serine‐PI family) has been shown to be induced in response to heat stress (Annamalai and Yanagihara,), however, information regarding PI‐II‐class role in HS is completely lacking.

evidence_context_sentences.text: We find there is enrichment of GO term of ‘serine‐type endopeptidase inhibitor activity’ in CA4‐specific up‐regulation (Figure 2c).

Silencing of PI‐II was confirmed by qRT‐PCR (Figure S11), and the leaves of knock‐down plants were upright, enduring HS better than those of vector control plants which showed drooping and burnt leaves (Figure 5a).
Moreover, there was appreciable difference phenotypically when VIGS was repeated in sensitive background (Pusa Ruby) (Figure 5b).
The CLN PI‐II knock‐down plants exhibited significant enhancement in WUEi and net photosynthetic rates as compared to TRV‐control plants under HS (Figure 5c).
In addition, both the transpiration rate and the stomatal conductance were lower in TRV‐PI‐II plants under HS (Figure 5c,d).

evidence_context_sentences.text: The CLN PI‐II knock‐down plants exhibited significant enhancement in WUEi and net photosynthetic rates as compared to TRV‐control plants under HS (Figure 5c).
In addition, both the transpiration rate and the stomatal conductance were lower in TRV‐PI‐II plants under HS (Figure 5c,d).

The PI‐II overexpression in CLN decreased the tolerance of the seedlings of this robust cultivar as evident from reduced hypocotyl length as well as survival rate under HS in comparison with vector control seedlings.
In contrast, VIGS‐based silencing of PI‐II in Pusa Ruby exhibited higher survival rate (Figure 5e–g).
Furthermore, enhanced expression of HS marker genes in PI‐II‐silenced plants confirmed its role as a negative regulator of HS tolerance (Figure 5h,i).

entities.selected_description: Any biological process that results in permanent cessation of all vital functions of a cell. A cell should be considered dead when any one of the following molecular or morphological criteria is met: (1) the cell has lost the integrity of its plasma membrane; (2) the cell, including its nucleus, has undergone complete fragmentation into discrete bodies (frequently referred to as apoptotic bodies). The cell corpse (or its fragments) may be engulfed by an adjacent cell in vivo, but engulfment of whole cells should not be considered a strict criteria to define cell death as, under some circumstances, live engulfed cells can be released from phagosomes (see PMID:18045538).

entities.evidence_preview: AtHSFA6a is induced in response to ABA, salt and drought and is responsible for the transcriptional activation of stress‐responsive gene DREB2a via ABA‐dependent signalling pathway (Hwang et al.,).
Overexpression of Arabidopsis receptor‐like kinase ERECTA (ER) leads to enhanced HS tolerance in Arabidopsis, rice and tomato with increased biomass (Shen et al.,).
The unfolded protein response (UPR) is a conserved response that is elicited by ER stress and is known to protect plants from adverse environmental stresses (Park and Park,; Wan and Jiang,).

entities.evidence_preview: Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,).

entities.selected_description: Any of a series of ribonucleoprotein complexes that contain snRNA(s) and small nuclear ribonucleoproteins (snRNPs), and are formed sequentially during the spliceosomal splicing of one or more substrate RNAs, and which also contain the RNA substrate(s) from the initial target RNAs of splicing, the splicing intermediate RNA(s), to the final RNA products. During cis-splicing, the initial target RNA is a single, contiguous RNA transcript, whether mRNA, snoRNA, etc., and the released products are a spliced RNA and an excised intron, generally as a lariat structure. During trans-splicing, there are two initial substrate RNAs, the spliced leader RNA and a pre-mRNA.

selected_concepts.label: spliceosomal complex
selected_concepts.description: Any of a series of ribonucleoprotein complexes that contain snRNA(s) and small nuclear ribonucleoproteins (snRNPs), and are formed sequentially during the spliceosomal splicing of one or more substrate RNAs, and which also contain the RNA substrate(s) from the initial target RNAs of splicing, the splicing intermediate RNA(s), to the final RNA products. During cis-splicing, the initial target RNA is a single, contiguous RNA transcript, whether mRNA, snoRNA, etc., and the released products are a spliced RNA and an excised intron, generally as a lariat structure. During trans-splicing, there are two initial substrate RNAs, the spliced leader RNA and a pre-mRNA.

entities.evidence_preview: Hierarchical clustering clearly divided the cultivars into four clades based on their HS response, viz. (i) most tolerant (CLN), (ii) moderately tolerant (Pusa Sadabahar, IIHR2274, Hisar Arun and IIHR2201), (iii) sensitive (Pusa Ruby, Pusa‐120 and Pusa Rohini) and (iv) highly sensitive (CA4) (Figure 1a).

Furthermore, proteins belonging to chaperones, winged helix TFs, mRNA splicing and processing factors were significantly enriched among the conserved up‐regulated genes (Figure S3c).

The highly HS‐responsive TF families with more than 20 of its members significantly modulated (up‐regulated and down‐regulated) at transcript level include bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, C3H, ERF, GRAS, HD‐ZIP, HSF, MYB, NAC and WRKY (Figure 3d).
